theorem_statement:
Let \(0<r_1<r_2<r_3\), and let \(f\) be holomorphic and nonvanishing on an open annulus containing

\[
{z\in\mathbb C:r_1\le |z|\le r_3}.
\]

For \(r\in[r_1,r_3]\), define

\[
M(r)=\max_{|z|=r}|f(z)|.
\]

Then

\[
\log M(r_2)
\le
\frac{\log(r_3/r_2)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_1)
+
\frac{\log(r_2/r_1)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_3).
\]

Equivalently, \(\log M(r)\) is convex as a function of \(\log r\).
raw_proof_text:
Let

\[
t_i=\log r_i
\qquad(i=1,2,3).
\]

Define the affine function \(L(t)\) on \([t_1,t_3]\) that interpolates the two endpoint values

\[
L(t_1)=\log M(r_1),
\qquad
L(t_3)=\log M(r_3).
\]

Explicitly,

\[
L(t)
=

\frac{t_3-t}{t_3-t_1}\log M(r_1)
+
\frac{t-t_1}{t_3-t_1}\log M(r_3).
\]

Since \(t_i=\log r_i\), this says

\[
L(\log r_2)
=

\frac{\log(r_3/r_2)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_1)
+
\frac{\log(r_2/r_1)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_3).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove

\[
\log M(r_2)\le L(\log r_2).
\]

Because \(f\) is holomorphic and nonvanishing, the function

\[
u(z)=\log |f(z)|
\]

is harmonic on the annulus. Also

\[
\log |z|
\]

is harmonic on the annulus since \(0\) is not in the annulus. Therefore

\[
v(z)=L(\log |z|)
\]

is harmonic on the annulus as an affine function of \(\log |z|\).

Consider

\[
w(z)=u(z)-v(z).
\]

This is harmonic on the annulus. We claim that \(w\le 0\) on the boundary circles \(|z|=r_1\) and \(|z|=r_3\).

If \(|z|=r_1\), then

\[
u(z)=\log |f(z)|\le \log M(r_1)=L(\log r_1)=v(z).
\]

Thus (w(z)\le 0) on the inner boundary circle. Similarly, if \(|z|=r_3\), then

\[
u(z)=\log |f(z)|\le \log M(r_3)=L(\log r_3)=v(z),
\]

so (w(z)\le 0) on the outer boundary circle.

By the maximum principle for harmonic functions on the closed annulus, \(w\le 0\) throughout the annulus. Hence for every \(z\) with \(r_1\le |z|\le r_3\),

\[
\log |f(z)|\le L(\log |z|).
\]

In particular, if \(|z|=r_2\), then

\[
\log |f(z)|\le L(\log r_2).
\]

Taking the maximum over all \(z\) with \(|z|=r_2\), we get

\[
\log M(r_2)\le L(\log r_2).
\]

Substituting the explicit formula for (L(\log r_2)) gives

\[
\log M(r_2)
\le
\frac{\log(r_3/r_2)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_1)
+
\frac{\log(r_2/r_1)}{\log(r_3/r_1)}\log M(r_3).
\]

This proves the theorem.
